1.blurb: The Scythians were the Celts' steppe counterparts — Iranian-speaking horse nomads who dominated the Pontic steppe north of the Black Sea during the same centuries the Hallstatt Celts were building their salt-mining wealth in the Alps. Both were Iron Age warrior aristocracies with spectacular burial traditions, but one rode horses across the grasslands and the other dug salt out of mountains.

While the Hallstatt Celts were establishing themselves in the Alpine heartland around 800-700 BCE, the Assyrian Empire was at its zenith — the most powerful military machine the world had yet seen, ruling from Nineveh with iron discipline and organized terror. The two civilizations never met, but they were exact contemporaries, building power on opposite ends of the ancient world.

Cyrus the Great founded the Achaemenid Empire in 559 BCE, conquering the Medes, Lydians, and Babylonians to build the largest empire the world had yet seen. While the Hallstatt Celts were burying their chiefs with Greek wine and gold torcs, Cyrus was absorbing entire civilizations into a single administrative system — two completely different models of power in the same century.

2.blurb: While Celtic artists were developing the swirling ambiguity of La Tene art around 450 BCE, Athens was in its golden age — Phidias sculpting the Parthenon, Sophocles writing tragedies, and Pericles leading the world's first democracy. Greek art pursued idealized naturalism; Celtic art pursued deliberate transformation. Two radically different ideas about what art is for.

3.blurb: Celtic mercenaries fought for Carthage in the Punic Wars alongside Numidians, Iberians, and Libyans — Hannibal's polyglot army at Cannae in 216 BCE included Gallic warriors who helped inflict Rome's worst defeat. The Carthaginians valued Celtic fighters for their size and ferocity, even if they complained about their indiscipline and tendency to loot rather than follow orders.

Alexander the Great's conquests between 336 and 323 BCE created the Hellenistic world that the Celts would invade a generation later. When Celtic ambassadors told Alexander they feared nothing except the sky falling, they were speaking to the most powerful man alive — and apparently did not care. His successors' wars fragmented his empire and created the power vacuum the Celts exploited.

4.blurb: Rome in 390 BCE was not yet the superpower it would become — just a medium-sized Italian city-state fighting its neighbors for survival. The Gallic sack traumatized the Romans so deeply that it became the foundational event of their military identity. Everything Rome built afterward — the walls, the legions, the obsession with readiness — was partly a response to the day Brennus threw his sword on the scale.

4.matchText: Scythians were at the height of their golden age
4.blurb: While the Celts were sacking Rome and raiding Delphi, the Scythians were enjoying their golden age on the Pontic steppe — producing the spectacular gold and electrum pieces from the Kul-Oba and Solokha kurgans. Both peoples terrified the classical world, but the Scythians stayed nomadic while the Celts settled in the lands they invaded.

When the Celts found a mountain path around the Greek defenders at Thermopylae in 279 BCE, they were repeating what the Persians had done in 480 BCE — Xerxes' army had used the same tactic to outflank Leonidas and his Spartans. Two barbarian invasions of Greece, two centuries apart, both bypassing the same narrow pass.

4.matchText: Spartans had held Xerxes in 480 BCE
4.blurb: The Battle of Thermopylae in 480 BCE, where Leonidas and his Spartans held the narrow pass against Xerxes' Persian army, became one of the defining moments of Greek identity. When the Celts invaded Greece in 279 BCE, they faced Greek defenders at the same pass and found the same mountain bypass the Persians had used.

5.blurb: The Vedic brahmins held a social position remarkably similar to the Celtic druids — a priestly intellectual class exempt from certain obligations, trained through years of memorization, wielding influence that cut across political boundaries. Both traditions descend from Indo-European-speaking cultures, and some scholars argue the druid-brahmin parallel reflects a common ancestral priestly institution.

5.matchText: Romans, who threw people to wild animals in the arena
5.blurb: The Romans who denounced Celtic human sacrifice as barbarism staged gladiatorial games where prisoners and slaves were killed for public entertainment — a spectacle that would eventually grow to industrial scale under the Empire. Roman moral outrage at druidic sacrifice was genuine propaganda, but the contrast between condemning ritual killing while celebrating arena killing reveals the selective nature of civilizational self-regard.

6.blurb: During the Warring States period, Chinese states were developing the same things the Celtic oppida represented — larger fortified towns, standardized coinage, professional bureaucracies, and more formal political institutions. The Chinese path led to Qin unification. The Celtic path was cut short by Roman conquest. Two parallel experiments in state-building, one completed, one interrupted.

7.blurb: Scipio Aemilianus destroyed Carthage in 146 BCE so thoroughly that the Romans plowed salt into the earth — and then turned the same siege expertise on the Celtiberian city of Numantia in 133 BCE. Rome's two most famous destructions within a single generation, one Phoenician and one Celtic, both ending in the defenders choosing death over surrender.

7.matchText: incorporate an area the size of France, Belgium, and western Germany into the Roman Empire
7.blurb: Caesar's conquest of Gaul transformed both Rome and Gaul forever. The plunder and glory funded Caesar's rise to dictatorship, triggered the civil wars that ended the Republic, and incorporated the entire Celtic heartland into the Roman world. Gaul became Rome's richest western province — but it took a million dead Gauls to get there.

Vercingetorix's scorched-earth strategy against Caesar was essentially the same tactic the Scythians had used to humiliate Darius I of Persia 460 years earlier — burn the land, deny the enemy food, refuse to fight on their terms. Whether Vercingetorix knew about Darius's failed invasion is unknown, but he independently arrived at the steppe nomads' signature insight: superior armies can be defeated by the landscape itself.

8.matchText: Emperor Claudius launched a full-scale invasion in 43 CE
8.blurb: The Roman conquest of Britain in 43 CE was the last great act of Roman expansion into the Celtic world. Claudius personally led the triumphal entry, parading war elephants through Camulodunum to mark the occasion. The invasion extended Roman power to the edges of the known world — and provoked Boudica's revolt barely seventeen years later.
